blurb:
Luke's story starts with his mom, Miss Daisy, a beautiful smaller shepherd whose owner unexpectedly passed away. The boyfriend couldn't deal with Miss Daisy and her two puppies, so he took all three to the county shelter and just left them there. The county held them for weeks hoping a family member would step up. Nobody did. Finally they put out a plea for rescue help.

Luke is the fun-loving, playful one of Miss Daisy's boys. He's on the smaller side for a shepherd, and his face has that Malinois look while his mom looks more like a German Shepherd. He got lucky because he arrived right when the rescue was running their summer camp, so he's been getting tons of attention and trail walks from kids all summer long.

This is a young dog who's been through some upheaval but came out of it with his sweet, loving personality intact. He's ready for a family who won't disappear on him. You can meet Luke at Our Pal's Place in Marietta.
scoring_notes:
• Tragic family loss story creates strong emotional appeal for readers
• Young shepherd with summer camp socialization appeals to active families
• Multi-week county hold plus rescue transfer suggests moderate shelter time

blurb:
His name is Pork Tenderloin and he's a little misunderstood. He likes attention, but only when he's in the mood for it. He'll come find you when he wants pets, but he's not going to put up with being grabbed or overwhelmed. Some cats are like this. They're not mean or antisocial. They just have opinions about when and how they want to be touched.

He needs a calm environment and someone who knows how to read cats. If you try to force affection on him, it's not going to work. But if you let him set the pace and respect his boundaries, he's got the potential to form a real bond with you. He's not a beginner cat, but for someone who understands felines, he could be something special.

Pork Tenderloin came to Good Mews from a county shelter for a better shot at finding the right home. He needs someone cat-savvy who appreciates that the best cats aren't always the easiest ones.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Hilarious name will grab reader attention and spark conversation
• Honest portrayal of boundary-setting cat appeals to knowledgeable owners
• 'Misunderstood' status suggests longer shelter stay than typical cats

blurb:
Her name is Tiny and it fits. She's petite and delicate, but don't mistake that for boring. She's curious about everything and loves exploring wherever she is. Whether she's chasing a toy, investigating a new corner of the house, or just settling in for some cozy time, she brings this gentle, playful energy with her.

Tiny came to Good Mews from a county shelter for a better chance at finding her home. She's got that perfect mix of playful and affectionate that makes cats so great to live with. Not too much energy, not too little. Just right.

If you're looking for a small cat who's going to be engaged with you and her surroundings without being overwhelming, Tiny might be exactly what you want. She's ready to find a home where she can keep exploring and being adored.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Perfect balance of playful and affectionate appeals to wide audience
• Easy-going personality makes her highly adoptable for most families
• Standard rescue story without urgency indicators suggests shorter wait

blurb:
Otis was on the county shelter's kill list not because he was aggressive or sick, but because he fell into the wrong date range. When the rescue team found him, he was so stressed in that loud, overcrowded place that he was literally peeing on himself. The second they got him into a quiet room, he buried his head in someone's lap and just stayed there. He needed out, and he knew it.

Now that he's had time to decompress, you can see who Otis really is. He's a fun-loving guy who remembers how to be a dog again. House training came back to him right away, along with sitting politely and wagging his tail. He loves attention and wants nothing more than to make his people happy. The stress response he had at the county shelter isn't who he is at all.

Whoever adopts Otis is getting a dog who knows what it's like to almost lose everything and will never take a good home for granted. He's going to be the most loyal, loving companion you could ask for. You can find him at Our Pal's Place in Marietta.
scoring_notes:
• Dramatic rescue from kill list creates urgent, emotional reader connection
• Transformation story shows resilience and gratitude that tugs at heartstrings
• Decompression period and stress recovery indicates extended shelter time

blurb:
Sometimes the best stories are about second chances. Beignet was adopted from Good Mews as a kitten, but somehow ended up scared and alone at a county shelter. Good Mews brought her back to safety, and now she's ready to try again.

She went straight into foster care to rebuild her confidence, and it worked. She's sweet, loves being petted, and is a social eater who likes having company at mealtime. When she wants attention, she'll sit close or lean against you. She can give gentle love bites when she gets overstimulated, so she needs someone who knows how to read cat signals and give her space when she asks for it.

This isn't a cat with behavioral problems. This is just a cat who's been through some stuff and needs an adopter who gets it. If you understand cats and want to give a good one her second shot at a real home, Beignet might be exactly what you're looking for.

Good Mews is a no-kill, cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. You'll need to submit an inquiry and visit in person to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Powerful second-chance story that readers will emotionally connect with
• Sweet, social cat who just needs understanding - appeals to experienced cat owners
• Foster care and rebuilding confidence suggests extended shelter time
